data.title: XPeng: The Pullback Is Almost Complete - Nearing The Buy Zone
data.description:
XPEV's Mass Market Launch & Autonomous Capabilities Support The Management's FY2025 Break Even Guidance

We previously covered XPeng (NYSE:XPEV) in April 2024, discussing why we had reiterated our Buy rating despite the stock's continuous underperformance compared to its peers.

With the management opting to enter the mass market model with an extremely attractive pricing point, further aided by its expansion to the wider Asia and Southeast Asian countries, we had believed that the automaker was likely to achieve accelerating sales from H2'24 onwards, significantly aided by its low break-even price-point and compelling XNGP ADAS platform.

XPEV YTD Stock Price

Since then, XPEV has mostly traded sideways before the meteoric rise since the end of September 2024, attributed to the Chinese government's stimulus measures "aimed at supporting the economy's" recovery and boosting the domestic consumer spending.

This stimulus has already triggered the immense recovery observed in numerous Chinese ADRs, along with Chinese automotive stocks, thanks to the country's supposed recovery "out of its deflationary funk."

With regards to the automotive sector, the Chinese government has recently doubled their subsidies to 20K Yuan by August 2024, for consumers whom scrap old cars and buy new EVs capped below the price point of 300K Yuan.

This is on top of the "extended the purchase tax breaks for NEVs until 2027" at 30K yuan per passenger vehicle, allowing the typically more expensive EVs to be more affordable for Chinese customers.

As a result of the potential boost in their sales, it is unsurprising that the market has gotten exuberant surrounding the Chinese automakers' intermediate term prospects, as evidenced by their robust September 2024 deliveries and MoM/QoQ growth acceleration.

If anything, XPEV's launch of the entry-level EV, MONA M03 at between 119.8K Yuan to 155.8K Yuan by late August 2024 has been highly strategic as well, attributed to the exemplary first month delivery number at over 10K units by September 2024.

This is on top of the robust overall delivery numbers of 21.35K EVs in September 2024 (+52.1% QoQ/+39.4% YoY) and YTD deliveries of 95.86K EVs (+17.7% YoY) - with the QoQ acceleration likely attributed to the well-received mass market model.

Readers must note that EVs below the price range of 200K Yuan remains the most popular segment domestically, as opposed to XPEV's previously premium product portfolio priced mostly above 200K Yuan - underscoring why BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF) has been able to dominate the EV market with its well diversified offerings priced between 72K Yuan for BYD Seagull to 1.68M Yuan for BYD Yangwang U9 (high-performance electric supercar).

These developments are material indeed, since the mass market model allows XPEV to drive profit margin improvements after the completion of its ongoing restructuring efforts, supply chain reforms, and the highly strategic partnership with Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY).

If anything, the XPEV management already hints at "stable and maintained at mid to low-teens" overall gross margins moving forward, despite the new mass market model launch, with it building upon the 14% observed in FQ2'24 (+1.1 points QoQ/+17.9 YoY) and the vehicle margin of 6.4% (+0.9 points QoQ/+15 YoY).

While the Chinese automaker remains unprofitable on an adj net income basis at margins of -15% (+5.9 points QoQ/+40.3 YoY), the gap has been narrowing drastically, with the management's guidance of breakeven sometime in 2025 seemingly plausible.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is also why we believe that the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather pessimistic, since we expect FY2024/FY2025 to be trough years with FY2026 to bring forth positive bottom-lines, albeit minimal.

This is especially since the XPEV management continues to reiterate the "iPhone 4 moment for autonomous driving" by latest 2026, with the automaker already aiming to release its "AI-powered XNGP-equipped mass-production smart EVs provide a driving experience equal to that of world-class Robotics by the second half of 2025" across 95% of China's roads.

This also builds upon Tesla's (TSLA) guidance of starting "unsupervised autonomous driving in Texas and California in 2025 for the firm's Model 3 and Model Y," as the autonomous driving capability is increasingly rolled out and accepted globally.

As a result of the growing revenues from services and others (including software) at $0.18B (+28.5% QoQ/+102.5% YoY), we believe that the consensus forward estimates are likely to upgraded in the intermediate term, once XPEV continues to execute the positive gross profit margins and narrowing net margin losses over the next few quarters.

XPEV Valuations

While XPEV may appear to be trading at a premium FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.57x, compared to the previous article at 0.50x and the sector median of 1.29x, we beg to differ.

Assuming that the automaker is able to generate the FY2025 revenues of $9.1B, it appears that the resultant EV/Sales valuations of 0.94x remain reasonable enough for long-sighted investors.

This is compared to its Chinese automaker peers, including BYD at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.12x, Li Auto (LI) at 0.77x, and NIO (NIO) at 1.33x, with it apparent that XPEV remains a compelling Buy after a moderate retracement.

At the same time, with FY2026 likely to bring forth positive profit margins, we believe that XPEV remains well capitalized to weather the near-term cash burn, based on the $5.14B of cash on balance sheet as of FQ2'24 (-10.2% QoQ/+10.5% YoY) - significantly aided by the strategic partnership with VWAGY.

Lastly, readers must not forget the potential boost from its rather speculative launch of its modular flying car, XPeng Aeroht in 2026, with the market likely closely watching the upcoming showcase in November 2024.

So, Is XPEV Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

XPEV 2Y Stock Price

For now, as discussed in our BYD article here, it is apparent that the recent rally has been overly done with most Chinese ADRs already returning part of their recent gains, XPEV included.

With the stock now retesting the historical support levels of $11 after failing to break out of the $13s resistance levels, we believe that further moderation is likely as traders unlock most of their gains at these inflated levels.

Despite so, we believe that the ongoing retracement is a gift indeed, since a further pullback to its next support levels of $9s and $10s is likely to trigger a far more attractive entry point, based on the estimated FY2025 EV/Sales valuations of 0.62x - with it bringing the stock nearer to its historical valuations and its peers.

As a result of its high-growth prospects along with the intermediate term tailwinds from the automotive subsidies, we are maintaining our Buy rating for the XPEV stock once the pullback is complete.

Risk Warning

It goes without saying that XPEV is only suitable for those with a long investing trajectory, since the automaker is only expected to record break even by FY2025 and minimal profitability in FY2026.

At the same time, readers may to monitor its upcoming execution, since the management has bet its long-term growth strategy on overseas markets - with it expected to play a "more significant role in driving both sales and profit growth," building upon the fact that overseas sales accounted for more than 10% of its total sales by the latest quarter.

The aggressive global expansion may potentially trigger moderate bottom-line headwinds, with XPEV expecting to enter UK, Australia, and Southeast Asia in H2'24, building upon its growing presence in the EU, Middle East, and LatAm - attributed to the projected doubling in its international sales stores (and likely operating expenses) over the next two quarters.

At the same time, readers must not forget that numerous countries are planning, or have imposed import tariffs for Chinese made EVs - with it potentially triggering demand headwinds and top/bottom-lines misses.

Combined with its Chinese ADR status and the potential volatility arising from the ongoing US elections through November 2024, it goes without saying that XPEV is only suitable for those with moderate geopolitical risk appetite.

data.title: Summit Midstream Makes A Game-Changing Acquisition
data.description:
Summit Midstream Corporation (NYSE:SMC) has taken the next step in its transformation as it has managed to survive the COVID-19 downturn and its impact on its business. At the same time, it has been able to deftly handle multiple almost bankruptcies. As we'll see throughout this article, the acquisition of Tall Oak Midstream III will enable future growth and returns.

Transaction Overview

The transaction is an expensive one, and disappointingly, it does come with hefty new equity issuance. That could hurt Summit Midstream's ability to drive future returns in its share price. Still, the cash enables SMC to attempt to manage that.

The acquisition consists of ~60% equity, 34% cash, and 6% earnout. The cash consideration is $155 million, and pro forma SMC current shareholders will have a 60% equity stake. The transaction is expected to close by the end of the year, and the total price is $425 million, with the potential for a $25 million earnout depending on performance. That would be on or before Q2 2026, and it's a profitable payout.

The pro forma business will be 49% natural gas with a strong basin footprint. It is banking on LNG driving strong U.S. natural gas demand and more stable prices, and betting on natural gas prices trending above $3.5/MMBtu. There is some risk to the transaction, prices need to remain at that level to justify a continued investment in the business. Still, in a world where that happens, the transaction is quite profitable.

They expect the pro forma company to be able to see strong growth and continued shareholder returns. EBITDA is expected to grow from ~$250 million to ~$350 million, while annual capex will decline down to $65 million. At the same time, they expect ~70% to ~80% FCF conversion. Specifically, FCF can grow from ~$180 million in 2024 to $290 million in 2028 PF.

That's important. It has $1 billion in debt, continuing to cost it almost $100 million in annual interest payments. At the same time, there is a pro forma market capitalization of almost $650 million. SMC has the potential to have a strong double-digit FCF yield; however, it needs that FCF to justify investing over the long term. Still, we expect it to pan out over the long term.

SMC expects 1.5x de-levering from 2024E to 2028E, primarily driven by EBITDA growth. Rolling over debt at a lower interest rate, combined with additional stability, could make it a valuable investment.

Summit Midstream Corporation Milestones

Summit Midstream has continued to hit a number of strategic milestones, more than doubling its share price over the last year. This was supported by the sale of its Utica position to clean up its balance sheet and continued investments.

Summit Midstream has converted to a C-Corp and managed to increase its liquidity and delay its maturities. It also partook in the Double E pipeline, gaining 10 years of take-or-pay contracts, and is working on both a potential midpoint compression project and >300 MMcf/d of additional contracts. This is a key point of potential cheap profit growth, so it's something worth paying close attention to.

Management is working to deleverage the portfolio, and its recent acquisition discussed above has substantial detail. SMC still needs to clean up its delayed preferred dividend, but whether it can deleverage and generate returns as expected is something to keep an eye on.

Summit Midstream Corporation Targets

There is one part of the forecast that we want to discuss within its potential risk targets.

Tall Oak customers have >300k dedicated lease acres, with >500 remaining locations. Breakevens are in line with 2025 strip pricing; however, 2025 natural gas prices and recent natural gas prices are incredibly weak. The question for the company's acreage is whether natural gas prices can stay higher than expected. Still, the asset has experienced ~55% in volume growth to 2023 with 65 well connections, and it needs to see volume remain strong.

Summit Midstream saw no well connects in 2024, an indication of the top market, and it saw a mere 10 connects in 2023. 2025 has ~35 forecast connects; however, SMC needs that to justify future shareholder returns. These targets are ambitious, and that's worth paying incredibly close attention to as an interested investor.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is two-fold.

The first is that many of the assets are still reliant on natural gas companies that don't operate on the same scale. Summit Midstream has struggled with low utilization for years, and that's a risk to the new assets and its ability to continue generating long-term shareholder returns.

The second is that SMC is making a massive acquisition that needs ~$3.1/MMBtu natural gas prices, which is >20% above current prices. It does have reasons to be optimistic about natural gas prices, and we don't disagree; however, if it's wrong about prices that could hurt the ability to drive returns.

Conclusion

Summit Midstream Corporation is a high-risk, high-reward investment opportunity. It's undergoing a major acquisition with Tall Oak, which with an equity consideration will push SMC past $600 million market capitalization. On top of that, it is spending almost $150 million in cash on the acquisition. The company is buying an investment that had no well connects in 2024, and it's a bet on rising natural gas prices.

Still, the management team has made a number of intelligent investment decisions in the past, and we expect it to continue doing so. They are cleaning up the balance sheet, which could enable it to generate a double-digit FCF ratio, and which could enable substantial shareholder returns. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Nvidia At All-Time Highs: Why I'd Pay $83
data.description:
Yesterday, NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) hit a new intraday all-time high of $140.87 on news that Bank of America Corporation (BAC) had upgraded its fiscal 2025 EPS estimate for the company to $2.87. Nvidia shares were also likely impacted by the release of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) aka TSMC's Q3 earnings, which beat expectations and triggered a 10% rally in that stock the day after they came out.

When I read about Bank of America's EPS raise and TSMC's earnings beat, my mind immediately turned to Nvidia. TSMC's release showed significant growth in revenue and EPS, and beat expectations on both metrics. This fact looked bullish for Nvidia. TSM is NVDA's contract manufacturer; therefore, the former's earnings beat and CAPEX guidance raise bodes well for the latter's revenue. That plus B of A upping its EPS estimate for the year got me feeling more bullish on Nvidia than I had before. News that Stanley Druckenmiller said he'd regretted his earlier decision to sell NVDA also contributed to this nascent bullishness.

That being said, a feeling is just a feeling, and I'm not the type of person to invest based on gut instincts. Whether a stock goes up long-term mostly has to do with whether the discounted value of its future cash flows is worth more than the current stock price; the investor's feelings have nothing to do with it.

So, I set out to determine what price exactly I'd be comfortable buying Nvidia at. After hashing out some assumptions, building a rudimentary Excel model, and then running the discounted cash flow ("DCF") math based on the model, I arrived at a price target:

$83.

$83 seemed to me a reasonable price for a conservative value investor to pay for Nvidia stock. Today, after re-reading my Excel model, I still think it is.

I'm not saying that a price higher than $83 for Nvidia is ridiculous. Some investors are pretty good at forecasting and extrapolating growth stories into the future, but that isn't my approach. As a value investor, I make assumptions that run against the bull thesis and only buy if the asset proves worth the investment under such punishing assumptions. In Nvidia's case, that means assuming that on-device AI sends some money in Apple Inc. (AAPL) and QUALCOMM Incorporated's (QCOM) direction, and that Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) eventually comes out with viable alternatives to Nvidia in the data center. We're not there yet, but it is prudent to assume that these two threats to Nvidia's moat become realities, lest we get carried away with overly rosy forecasts.

When I last covered Nvidia, I rated it a hold because of its mix of a steep valuation and very fast growth. This has been the theme of my Nvidia coverage just about since I started it. Given the current stock price, I am still not prepared to buy NVDA stock. But this week's flurry of bullish news for the company has got me thinking about it to the point where I have, for the first time ever, settled on an entry price at which I'd start buying. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will explain in detail why I'd buy NVDA if it declined in price to $83.

How Much Will NVIDIA Earn in the Future?

Before you can value a stock, you need some idea of how much the underlying company will earn in the future, and in what timeframe. An 'X' P/E ratio means entirely different things for a company that trades at 'X' and will continue growing rapidly, and a company that trades at 'X' with no growth prospects. The ratio of price to lifetime earnings is actually lower for the former company. The reason P/E ratios are given relative to one year's earnings (either last year or the coming year) is because forecasting future growth is difficult. Nevertheless, if you spend enough time thinking about it, you can come up with a fairly accurate forecast and, if you do so, you can feel justified in using metrics like the PEG (price/earnings/growth) ratio.

The PEG ratio gives us one clue about why people keep buying Nvidia stock despite all of its other multiples being sky-high: this specific ratio is only 0.15. Investors generally prefer to buy when the PEG ratio is below one, so Nvidia passes that test easily.

However, the problem with using the PEG ratio indiscriminately is that you can't just assume high growth will persist for no reason. You need to have a specific idea of how much the company will earn in the future; only then can you decide whether its growth will continue.

So, we need to look at some factors that will determine how much Nvidia earns in the future.

Competitive Landscape

One thing that Nvidia has going for it right now is a strong competitive position. It has a market share between 75% and 90% in AI accelerator chips; its chips are integrated with its CUDA computing platform which raises switching costs; and finally, developers optimizing their software for Nvidia gives the company a network effect where new apps have to be optimized for Nvidia to be compatible with other apps that use Nvidia. That's two of the four main moat sources right there, and Nvidia has various patents as well, so in truth it probably has three out of four.

However, there are some signs that Nvidia's moat may not endure for long. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) has a Tensor Processing Unit that Apple used instead of Nvidia chips for training Apple Intelligence. Cerebras AI has a computing platform with over a trillion transistors that is reportedly 20 times faster than the H100. Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) M1325X chipset is widely considered a viable Nvidia alternative. So far, these various developments haven't eaten into Nvidia's market share, but the technical advancements they represent are real. Probably, with enough funding, they will begin eating into Nvidia's moat, which will reduce its pricing power.

Power of Suppliers

Another factor in the durability of Nvidia's moat is the power of its suppliers. The biggest one of these is TSMC, and its power is considerable. TSMC is a large supplier to Nvidia, and its competitors don't have anywhere near the same capacity. Although Nvidia relies on Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) for some processors, it can't get its entire order book filled without TSMC. Nvidia is 11% of TSMC's business at least, and that 11% is more than half the size of Samsung's entire foundry revenue stream. It seems like Nvidia needs TSMC to keep delivering GPUs to customers, and most industry experts expect Nvidia to accept TSMC's coming price hikes.

Valuation

Having explored the two most important aspects of Nvidia's future prospects, I'm now ready to model its future earnings.

Here are the assumptions:

  • Revenue will grow at 111% year-over-year by the end of this year compared to fiscal 2024, a rate consistent with B of A's earnings forecast and TSMC's CAPEX guidance.

  • Next year, the growth rate will slow to 70% mainly due to base effects (i.e., continued growth being harder after a previous high-growth period).

  • The year after that, AMD and other companies' competing products started going mainstream, resulting in Nvidia's growth slowing to 20%.

  • In the next three years, the growth rate will slow from 10% to 6% reflecting rising competitive pressures and increasing base effects.

  • COGS, operating expenses and interest/taxes combined, all grow at rates commensurate with the revenue growth in the same year. This is consistent with the past trend in Nvidia's business.

These assumptions produce the following income statement model:

REVENUE

$129,074.26

$219,426.24

$263,311.49

$289,642.64

$312,814.05

$331,582.90

COGS

$31,003

$52,705.10

$63,246.12

$69,570.73

$75,136.39

$79,644.57

SG&A

$18,203

$30,945.10

$37,134.12

$40,847.53

$44,115.33

$46,762.25

EBIT

$79,868.26

$135,776.04

$162,931.25

$179,224.38

$193,562.33

$205,176.07

INTEREST & TAX

$10,000

$17,000.00

$20,400.00

$22,440.00

$25,689.31

$69,868.26

$4.44

​​As you can see, our model has EPS gradually growing from the $2.84 base amount to $7.66. The starting amount of $2.84 is consistent with B of A's $2.87 estimate, but a little lower.

Without discounting, the five annual amounts sum to $32.15. As for the discounted value of these amounts:

According to Statista, the market risk premium in the U.S. is 5.5%. Adding that to the current 10-year (US10Y) treasury yield (4.09%) gives us a 9.59% discount rate. Valuing the EPS figures above at this rate produces $24.15 in present value for the cash flows in the discrete forecast period.

Next, we need a steady state growth rate estimate. This is always challenging to come up with. However, we know that the long-term U.S. GDP growth rate is 3.21%, and that a company logically can't grow faster than the economy forever. Otherwise, it would end up bigger than the economy. So I'll put Nvidia's steady state growth rate at 2%, comfortably below GDP growth but not nothing. With this assumption, Nvidia's final forecasted cash flow is $7.82, and that's worth $102.98 discounted at 9.59% minus 2%. This $102.98, discounted back to the present, is worth $59.44. And finally, $59.44 plus $24.15 for the discrete forecast period is worth $83.59. To err on the side of caution, I'll round down instead of up, and call $83 the price at which I'd buy NVIDIA.

The Bottom Line

This week, investor enthusiasm about Nvidia reached a fever pitch. Driven by TSMC's blowout numbers, B of A's EPS estimate and Druckenmiller's regret over his sale, the stock soared to new heights. For me, the enthusiasm was contagious enough to get me digging into the numbers to locate a clear price target. Ultimately, the $83 target I got was far below the current price. But perhaps in a future recession or 2022-style tech sector downturn, I'll get it. As for those buying at today's prices: I think they're taking a big risk, but I wouldn't take the opposite side of their trading by shorting Nvidia.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oil Prices May Be Heading For A Major Breakdown
data.description:
Oil prices (CL1:COM) are making a second move lower and are again within striking distance of that all-important support level at $66, which goes back years. The last time oil made a move down to $66 was in early September. That time, it survived and rallied back to roughly $78.50, which helped push interest rates in the US higher and weaken the Japanese yen.

As noted previously, the $66 price has acted as a support and resistance level since 2017 and has been acting as such again more recently. A break of support in oil at $66 would probably not be a positive signal for global growth, especially given how much China is working towards stimulating its economy presently.

Global Implications

During periods of strong global growth, one would think that oil demand rises as energy needs increase. Likewise, during periods of economic slowdown, demand would fall. However, the current scenario with falling oil prices suggests that demand is not solid or that there is potential oversupply.

The odd thing is that China, which has the second-largest GDP, is trying to get its economy going again, but oil prices are still not responding positively. This would either suggest that the oil market simply thinks that China's measures are not sufficient to help the Chinese economy, or that growth in the rest of the world won't have a strong effect on meeting supply.

What is even stranger is that the US economy is supposed to be very strong as well, with the Atlanta Fed GDPNow model indicating real growth of 3.4% in the third quarter.

This raises questions about oil's weakness and could suggest that the oil market does not see future global growth as sufficient to meet supply.

Approaching Support

The technical charts show that if oil were to break $66 on this second attempt, it could lead to its dropping to around $57. Certainly, momentum in oil has been bearish as measured by the relative strength index. Additionally, as measured by the RSI, oil is not currently oversold, and it still has room to fall, given that the lower Bollinger band is around $66, also suggesting that oil is not oversold.

Another technical point is that oil fell out of a symmetrical trading pattern in early September. In October, it retested the breaking of that technical pattern and failed, turning lower, which would suggest that a retest of $66 is likely coming.

Impacts on Rates And The Japanese Yen

The move lower in oil could result in 10-year rates moving lower, as the two have been highly correlated over time. This is partially because of oil's impacts on energy prices and, thus, on US inflation rates.

Additionally, the USD-JPY (USD:JPY) is highly correlated to oil prices and the 10-year rate. It weakened from around 140 in September to 150 in October as oil prices rose and interest rate differentials widened. But should oil move lower and 10-year rates adjust accordingly, then spreads will likely begin to narrow again, leading to the USD-JPY strengthening.

The last time oil was trading at around $69, the USD-JPY was trading at around 140, versus its current 149.50. The technical setup in the USD-JPY also suggests that it fall back to 140. The pattern shows a pole with an upsloping flag, which can be a bearish pattern that breaks lower. It would suggest that the USD-JPY return to 141.50.

A stronger yen would also make sense, given its relationship with lower oil prices over time. Since Japan mainly imports energy, rising oil prices can hurt economic growth, weaken the yen, or help the economy when prices fall.

Final Impacts

Of course, if oil prices continue to hold support at $66, then the commodity will again survive and likely see a sharp rebound to the mid-to-upper $70s, pushing higher rates and likely leading to the strengthening of the yen.

At present, the odds appear to favor a break of support, just given the technical setups and the fact that oil doesn't seem to be as oversold this time as it was last time.

data.title: Trump Vs. Harris: 7 Election Stocks To Position Your Portfolio For Q4
data.description:
Mixed Sentiment in October

The US Presidential Election is less than a month away, and a number of market themes have evolved since we last covered the election in August, when Vice President Kamala Harris became the Democratic nominee for president.

Concerns over weakening job growth that led to heightened volatility in the markets in early August have abated, following a strong September jobs report, but that could have implications for those expecting another rate cut in November. Inflation has further cooled in the interim period, though the September CPI report declined less than expected. Geopolitical tensions have only increased, with Iran's missile attack on Israel leading to elevated energy prices, which until the week of September 30th had lagged all other sectors in returns YTD, but now lead sectors on a trailing one-month basis.

S&P 500 Sector Returns

The other factor impacting markets is the potential outcome of the US Presidential Election.

Harris vs. Trump

In July, Harris had succeeded in narrowing the lead Trump previously held over President Joe Biden, prior to his dropping out of the race. According to the Real Clear Politics (RCP) poll of polls average, Harris is currently ahead of Trump by 1.7%.

However, polls in recent elections have proven to be unreliable. In 2020, polls had the largest errors in 40 years.

Quant Removes the Emotion

Like markets trading on sentiment, polls are vulnerable to short-term swings based on emotions or immediate news, which can lead to unpredictable outcomes. In contrast, American Historian Allan Lichtman's model, 13 Keys to the White House, focuses on underlying fundamentals based on the incumbent party's performance rather than fluctuating sentiment. Lichtman's model identifies 13 true or false "keys" or statements and if five or fewer statements are false, the incumbent is predicted to win. Lichtman's model has been successful in predicting nine out of the last ten elections since 1984. In 2024, Lichtman's model predicts Harris to win.

Lichtman's model is similar to SA Quant's "Quantamental" approach where stocks' attributes like growth, value, or profitability are assigned grades vs. the peer sector that are then weighted to maximize the predictive value. The best stocks are awarded a 'Strong Buy' rating. This can help investors analyze their portfolios free from emotion to maximize their chances of making gains.

Harris vs. Trump Sectors

Kamala Harris replaced Joe Biden as the Democratic presidential nominee, and analysts have speculated on whether she will continue pursuing Biden's policies. Harris also offers the possibility of a Silicon Valley Commander-in-Chief with more progressive leanings on green energy and technology, whereas Trump's 2024 proposals and focus call for modernizing the military and increasing domestic manufacturing, and he's overturned restrictions on big banks.

Kamala Harris

Based on Kamala Harris' previous governing record, regional ties, and campaign agenda, SA Quant has identified three sectors that could potentially benefit from a Harris presidency.

Information Technology

Harris has received numerous endorsements from tech industry leaders, with a few notable exceptions and has deep ties to the region from her time as a California Attorney General and Senator. In her experience as a Senator, her record showed a balanced stance between growth and responsible governance in the tech sector.

Alternative Energy

The flagship piece of legislation from the Biden Administration, the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) allocates substantial funding for clean energy initiatives, and as President, Harris would oversee the implementation of these initiatives. Harris has highlighted the importance of expanding clean energy manufacturing jobs such as solar panel and wind turbine production, and domestic alternative energy manufacturers stand to benefit.

Industrials

Harris supported the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and IRA, which includes $30B for electric grid infrastructure and has a focus on domestic manufacturing that could benefit industrials across various subsectors.

Donald Trump

Donald Trump's Agenda 47, a collection of formal policy plans, and his policies in his previous administration suggest that the sectors below would benefit from a Trump presidency.

Aerospace and Defense

Defense procurement increased 24.8% between 2017-2020 compared with the previous administration and Trump's current agenda advocates for military readiness which would likely be a boon for the aerospace and defense sectors.

Financials

In Trump's first term, he was successful in rolling back several Dodd-Frank provisions, easing the regulatory burden of financial institutions. A continuation of these policies could help boost profitability for financials.

Construction and Engineering

Trump's Agenda 47 aims to charter 10 new cities on undeveloped federal land and streamline approvals for infrastructure projects, potentially catalyzing demand for construction and engineering firms and benefiting the sector.

Harris and Trump Shared Agenda

Both Harris and Trump have plans in place that could benefit the homebuilding sector. Harris has cited her support for building 3 million new homes and offering down payment assistance to address the affordable housing shortage. At the same time, Trump has expressed support for cutting back on zoning and permitting requirements for real estate developers, which could accelerate the construction of new housing developments. The homebuilding sector is one that stands to win regardless of who wins in November.

Election Stocks to Watch

As the presidential election nears, we feature seven U.S. stocks spanning sectors described above with meaningful earnings growth data, solid investment fundamentals, and are Quant-rated 'Buy' or 'Strong Buy.'

1. Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (ZETA)

  • Sector: Information Technology.

  • Market Capitalization: $7.12B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 3 out of 552.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 1 out of 191.

Zeta provides a cloud platform that leverages AI to streamline marketing efforts for companies across 15 verticals, including retail, financial services, and telecom. As a top quant-rated application software stock, ZETA has had a phenomenal year, outperforming the broader market and tech sector (XLK) alike, and was also an Alpha Picks selection in August. Growth in Q2 was supported by rebounds in both the automotive and insurance industries, contributing to an A- Growth grade with key highlights including 25% yoy revenue growth and an EPS FWD long-term growth rate (3-5Y CAGR) of +34%. This has translated to strong profitability, evidenced by a gross profit margin of 60%, which is a +22% increase over the sector median, and a levered free cash flow margin of 14%.

The company has maintained exceptional momentum, delivering 'A+' grades over the last four quarters, and as such the stock's value profile has diminished somewhat. However, Zeta P/E Growth (PEG) FWD, a heavily weighted valuation metric of 1.61x indicates the stock is trading at a 16% discount to the sector. Wall Street consensus optimism has coalesced around Zeta with eight FY1 up revisions in the last 90 days, and two downward revisions, and the company's earnings are projected to grow 33% by fiscal year-end. Zeta is one tech stock with solid fundamentals and favorable industry tailwinds that could push it to new heights under a potential Harris administration

2. First Solar, Inc. (FSLR)

  • Sector: Information Technology (Alternative Energy).

  • Market Capitalization: $22.00B.

  • Quant Rating: Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 88 out of 552.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 9 out of 66.

First Solar Inc. is a US-based solar technology company that designs, manufactures, and sells photovoltaic solar modules and systems. The company is undergoing rapid expansion in its manufacturing capacity, with recently completed facilities in Ohio, and Alabama, and plans to complete a factory in Louisiana that will be operational in H2 2025. The company is positioned to benefit from tailwinds from recently imposed duties on solar imports. FSLR's growth highlights include 26% yoy revenue growth and 133% FWD EBITDA growth. The company has been able to convert that into an exceptional 43% EBITDA margin, which is a 340% increase over the sector median. The company has also been able to generate an extraordinary 1.19B in cash from operations.

FSLR Stock Growth Grade

The company has plenty of value to offer investors, despite the solid 36% price performance it's delivered over the trailing one-year period. FSLR is currently trading at 18x earnings and 2.9x book value, a 25% and 11% reduction to the sector median respectively. FSLR beat revenue expectations by more than $76M dollars and EPS estimates by $0.59 in Q2. FSLR's tremendous growth, excellent value, and positive earnings make this a potential alternative energy play for a Harris administration.

3. Powell Industries, Inc. (POWL)

  • Sector: Industrials.

  • Market Capitalization: $3.36B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 3 out of 625.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 1 out of 65.

Powell Industries, Inc. builds complex, custom-engineered electrical solutions for clients in the industrial and energy sectors including utilities, transportation, oil, and LNG end markets. Powell is #1 among quant-rated electrical component and equipment stocks and a two-time Alpha Picks selection. POWL soared after beating top and bottom line expectations for Q3 of its FY 2024, with revenue climbing +50% YoY and gross profit +92%, driven by strong growth in the oil and gas sectors, and a significant increase in new orders from the electric utility sector. POWL has been able to sustain its momentum since its earnings release, and has delivered 83% in the three-month period. Powell's explosive profitable growth is expected to continue with consensus estimates projecting FY24 EPS to rise 123% and revenue +30%. Powell outperforms industrial sector profitability with a net income margin of 14% and levered FCF margin of 17%.

POWL Stock Growth Grade

Powell outperforms industrial sector profitability with a net income margin of 14% and a levered FCF margin of 17%. The company is still trading at a valuation that is in line with the sector, and given this stock's solid fundamentals and increasing demand for electrical solutions in an era of aging energy infrastructure, Powell is well-positioned to continue to maintain its solid market position.

4. Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC)

  • Sector: Aerospace and Defense.

  • Market Capitalization: $77.72B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 23 out of 625.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 3 out of 59.

Northrop Grumman Corporation is a major American aerospace and defense company headquartered in Falls Church, VA. NOC is best known for its development and manufacturing of a broad range of military defense equipment, including aircrafts, drones, missile defense systems, and ammunition. However, the company is also heavily involved in the development of civilian and commercial space technologies, including satellite systems. NOC is one of the largest defense contractors in the US, and also supplies international customers. The company has a massive backlog of $83B with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.5x. In Q2, NOC raised its full year guidance for 2024 on the back of a 7% sales increase, with growth in all four of its main business segments.

NOC is exceptionally profitable, underpinned by $4.3B in cash from operations and an extraordinary 22.34 in cash per share vs. the sector median of 2.08. It also has strong valuation characteristics, in particular its FWD PEG of 0.72, which is a -63% reduction vs. the sector median, as well as TTM dividend yield of 1.48% which is +12% above the sector. Wall Street has expressed consensus optimism around NOC with a whopping 22 FY1 up revisions, and zero down revisions in the last 90 days. This company's formidable market position, innovative products that span both the civilian and military sectors and excellent fundamentals make NOC a solid pick for a Trump win.

5. First Citizens BancShares, Inc. (FCNCA)

  • Sector: Financials.

  • Market Capitalization: $29.36B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 27 out of 682.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 6 out of 66.

First Citizens Bancshares Inc. is a top Quant-rated diversified bank that primarily operates through its main subsidiary First-Citizens Bank & Trust Company throughout the southeast, Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, and Western parts of the US. FCNCA offers a wide range of banking products and services including traditional retail, wealth management, commercial, and digital banking. FCNCA acquired Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) after its collapse in March 2023, and the acquisition has proven fruitful, helping drive growth for the company's loan portfolio which grew at an annualized rate of 11.8% in Q2 2024. FCNCA sports an overall 'A+' growth grade, with forward revenue growth at 29% which represents a 432% increase above the sector median. This has translated to solid profitability with a net income margin of 29%, which exceeds the sector by 28% and $2.9B in cash from operations. FCNCA beat revenue expectations by $44.2M in Q2 and blew past EPS estimates by $6.12, resulting in 10 FY1 upward revisions in the last 90 days and zero down revisions.

FCNCA Earnings Revision Grade

These results have helped drive the company's 'A-' momentum grade, and despite the gains, the company is trading at an attractive valuation at 10.5x FWD earnings vs. the sector's 12.7x. FCNCA scores well above average across all factor grades and is an excellent choice within the financials sector for a potential Trump win.

6. Argan, Inc. (AGX)

  • Sector: Industrials.

  • Market Capitalization: $1.75B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 4 out of 625.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 1 out of 32.

Argan is a top Quant-rated engineering and construction company that provides services to the power, industrial construction, and telecom infrastructure sectors. AGX operates through multiple subsidiaries, both domestically and abroad. In Q2, AGX saw both top and bottom-line growth, with revenue increasing 61% compared with the previous year, totaling $227M, and an EBITDA growth of 21%. This translates to strong profitability for the company, with an ROE of nearly 15% and an exceptional levered free cash flow margin of 21%, a more than 200% increase relative to the sector median.

Argan exceeded revenue estimates by $36M in Q2 and delivered a positive EPS surprise of $0.34. Wall Street analysts have also expressed a bullish sentiment for AGX, with two upwardly revised FY1 estimates in the last 90 days and zero downward revisions.

AGX Momentum Grade

AGX's extraordinary price momentum has been consistent across quarters, and while its value profile has been somewhat diminished as a result, the company is still delivering on key metrics like its price-to-cash flow ratio of 10x which is a 33% reduction to the sector median. With a project backlog exceeding $1B and the ability to service traditional and renewable energy projects alike, AGX is poised for future growth opportunities.

7. Green Brick Partners, Inc. (GRBK)

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary (Homebuilding).

  • Market Capitalization: $3.73B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 12 out of 497.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2024): 1 out of 24.

Green Brick Partners is a diversified homebuilder and land development company that operates in Texas, Georgia, and Florida. Driven by inventory remaining near historic lows, Green Brick home closings rose 26% YoY to a record level of 987, revenue +20% to $547M, while gross margins reached an all-time high of 33.4%. Green Brick says it is strategically poised to capitalize on long-term secular tailwinds, including among the 35 to44year-old demographic, a segment expected to generate strong demand. Green Brick's strategy and land provide a potential advantage to continue gaining market share in a capital-efficient manner, CEO James Brickman said in an earnings call.

Green Brick beat earnings in seven of the past eight quarters and EPS is projected to grow nearly 36% in FY24 and revenue +18%. Green Brick showcases impressive profitability attributes, including an EBIT margin of 22% and a net income margin of 17% which is a nearly +300% increase relative to the sector median. Green Brick maintains attractive valuation characteristics, including a 10x FWD P/E ratio, which represents a -47% reduction vs. the sector and a PEG of 0.43. GRBK is the top Quant-rated homebuilding stock and is one company that likely stands to benefit, no matter who wins the race in November.

Concluding Summary

As the 2024 US Presidential election inches closer, the race between Kamala Harris and Donald Trump remains close, and polls can be unreliable predictors of election outcomes. Markets have been plagued by a multitude of uncertainties around the macroeconomic climate, monetary policy, geopolitical tensions, and of course, the outcome of the election. SA Quant has identified potential winning sectors for each candidate and presents seven stocks to watch across these sectors. These companies are rated as 'Buy' or 'Strong Buy' by SA Quant, demonstrate strong fundamentals, growth potential, favorable industry, and market positioning. Notably, the homebuilding sector could benefit regardless of the election outcome.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into desired sectors you like. Or, if you're seeking a limited number of monthly ideas, consider exploring Alpha Picks.

data.title: Fraport Stock: I Am Downgrading To Hold On Higher Risks To Growth
data.description:
In June, I covered Fraport AG (OTCPK:FPRUF), (OTCPK:FPRUY) and reiterated my buy rating on the stock. However, the stock price development has not been spectacular. From June to August, the ordinary shares registered listed on the Frankfurt home exchange (FWB:FRA) declined from €54 to €44 before recovering to €48.80 at the time of writing. That is not the kind of performance we are looking for.

In this report, I will discuss whether Fraport remains a buy in my view or whether a hold or even a sell rating is more justified given the lackluster performance.

Fraport Performance Is Strong, Despite Challenged At Frankfurt Airport

Second quarter revenues grew 14% to €2 billion, with EBITDA growing 18% to €567 million. Frankfurt Airport, even though it experienced some challenges, accounted for around half of the growth in revenues. The international airports drove the remainder of the growth. Fraport Greece recorded a €22.2 million increase, with €25.5 million for Lima in Peru and €33 million for Fraport USA as Fraport won a contract for management of retail at Dulles and Ronald Reagan Airport. Those growth drivers essentially were also the growth drivers in the first quarter of the year.

The aviation segment saw revenues increase by 15.4% driven by higher volumes and higher fees charged for services provided by Fraport. Personnel expenses increased 8.4% supporting the higher volumes while EBITDA grew to €165.5 million indicating a 31.6% increase. In retail and real estate, the revenues increased 6.8% driven by volume and slightly higher retail revenue per passenger. The EBITDA, however, remained flat at around €180 million due to temporary elevated maintenance costs. Furthermore, we noted that advertising revenues picked up in the second quarter, but retail revenues from shopping were a bit soft.

Ground handling has been a challenging part of the business, where the goal is to reach a break-even level. In the first half of the year, the revenues grew 12.7% to €353.5 million, and EBITDA losses improved from €36.7 million to €23.8 million, driven by price increases and higher volumes. The second quarter shows an improvement in EBITDA from a €12.6 million loss to a €3.9 million loss, which I believe shows good progress in reaching break-even levels eventually.

The international business, which includes the retail services to the US airports and the international airports that are being operated, posted a 17.1% increase in revenues with EBITDA growth of 15.2% to €245.5 million. Keeping in mind that the airports in Brazil are impacted by the floodings earlier this year and the financial turmoil at Brazilian airline GOL, the performance of the international portfolio shows that the company is nicely diversified.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Fraport?

The opportunities for Fraport are obviously the growing demand for air travel. However, we also see some risks that Fraport has to manage. The bar chart above shows that the recovery of passenger traffic has not been uniform, and that also means that there still is significant growth opportunity for some airports in the Fraport portfolio.

Frankfurt was impacted earlier this year by strikes in Germany. Furthermore, some growth that would normally be realized from Frankfurt will not be realized for now due to delivery delays of commercial airplanes that are affecting Lufthansa as well as many other airlines. Additionally, climate protests at airports in Western Europe are posing a risk to operations that could significantly impact results. Weak demand for travel to and from China is also having an effect on aviation as well as the retail side of Frankfurt Airport. That brings us to the overarching risk that Fraport, like any other company, is not completely shielded from macroeconomic headwinds.

Furthermore, adverse weather could impact the operations of Fraport in the short-term as in case of natural disasters airports could be closed. Insurance coverage and rebalancing charges do offset some earnings and revenue risk, but it is still something to keep in mind. Another risk for Fraport is a decline in demand for travel to and from certain airports. The Twin Star airports heavily depend on demand for holidays at Sunny Beach in Bulgaria, and it does seem like demand for those holidays is declining. There is soft demand from Germany and Poland for those airports, which is also triggering Wizz Air to remove some airplanes from its base in Varna.

Fraport Stock Downgraded To Hold

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Fraport, I have changed the base from which the price targets are calculated. Previously, this was based on the ticker FPRUY which is the ADR representing one ordinary share on the OTC market while FPRUF represents the ordinary shares on the OTC. Both of these tickers lack volume. As a result, I do believe that it is better to buy Fraport stock on the stock exchange of Frankfurt where it trades under the symbol FRA. There it has a significantly higher volume, allowing for buying and selling at desired prices and quantities, and better price-making.

Assessing the price target for Fraport is tricky. The reason is that the company has an EV/EBITDA multiple that is significantly below that of peers, and I actually don't see an excellent reason for that discount. A peer group valuation would lead to a price target of $64.41 indicating a 23% upside for 2024 and a 34% upside for 2025. At the same time, there are some risks regarding the recovery of Frankfurt. This is due to pressures that Lufthansa is facing and the flights to and from China. While I don't believe that justifies a discount of Fraport stock compared to peers, I do believe it will keep the stock price somewhat lower. A hold rating is justified until we see some risk factors being mitigated or overcome by growth in other parts of the business.

Conclusion: Fraport Is A Hold

While I am downgrading Fraport AG stock from buy to hold, I continue to like the longer-term prospects of the company. However, I also remain mindful about the risks and the CapEx profile which keeps debt at elevated levels, preventing a sustainable dividend payment until debt is reduced. So, for now, I am downgrading the stock to hold as we await a stronger recovery at Frankfurt, a reduction in CapEx, and the full reopening of the closed airport in Brazil.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: Gathering Energy For The Next Run
data.description:
In my previous article on Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH), I mentioned how much I liked the company’s growth prospects. Despite being somewhat pricey valuation-wise, I felt that Celsius was growing rapidly enough that it could grow into its valuation. Since then, the company has been hit with a slew of negative news, plunging the stock -60% from the time I wrote that piece. However, I believe that this dip could represent a buying opportunity for long-term investors.

Issues Facing CELH Stock

There are basically two issues putting a damper on the enthusiasm for CELH stock. Both of these concerns are related to Revenue growth, something which is particularly important to Celsius as a high-growth company. The first major concern is related to Celsius’ ability to gain market share from the larger energy drink companies, namely Red Bull and Monster (MNST).

In the latest Nielsen survey, as reported in Seeking Alpha, Celsius’ sales growth over a four-week period increased by 5.6%. This is much lower than the 7.2% tracked over a 12-week period (roughly 3 months). The decline in sales growth between the 3-week and the 12-week was attributed to a decline of 6% in Celsius product prices.

Overall, Celsius’ market share has consistently been 9.5% to 9.7% over the last two months. However, when looking strictly at volume, Celsius already has about a 10.3% market share. There is some concern that Celsius' popularity may have peaked and that it will remain a player with about a 10% market share. As noted by TD Cowen analyst Robert Moskow;

We believe that the sequential slowdown in market share gains in recent periods is the result of increased promo activity by peers, as well as successful sugar-free innovation by Red Bull

The second issue weighing down on Celsius’ stock is the slowdown in the Energy drink market. This slowdown is primarily being driven by macroeconomic factors, i.e. a slowing US economy is putting pressure on consumer spending. Convenience store sales are being particularly hit hard. Foot traffic in convenience stores could be down as much as 3% to 3.5%.

In the long term, however, the US energy drinks market size could still grow at a rapid CAGR of 7.1% from 2024 to 2030 from its current size of $23.4 billion. I believe that Celsius’ brand and unique product could capture a lot of that market growth.

Celsius is positioned well on long-term trends

The Nielsen data is quite interesting to use as a gauge / directional feel on consumer sentiment for the Celsius brand. The latest data points to stagnating growth for the company, at least in the short term. However, I do not believe that obsessing over the short-term Nielsen data is helpful for investors with a longer time horizon.

Rather, I like to look into longer-term trends. In my view, I believe that Celsius is well-positioned to capture these trends identified by Nielsen. The largest of which is the trend toward Health and Wellness for Energy Drinks. There is a growing focus on living healthier. This translates to consumers exercising more, which benefits energy drinks, particularly those high in caffeine. In fact, searches for the words “high caffeine” saw a 113% increase compared to last year.

In conjunction with looking for a caffeine kick, health-conscious consumers are also looking to reduce the sugar in their diets. According to Nielsen, 30% of consumers seek low sugar, and 29% look for no added sugar when purchasing food and drinks.

These trends bode well for Celsius, which has positioned itself as a “healthier” energy drink with roughly the same amount of caffeine as its competitors. Celsius' brand image is unique compared to Red Bull or Monster, as evidenced by the drink being popular among young women and Gen-Z.

Energy drinks like Monster and Red Bull marketed themselves as being edgy and extreme – traits that are popular with young men. Red Bull being affiliated with Formula race car driving and the Claw marks on Monster cans are a clear giveaway. An article from The Guardian describes this best;

Skinny white cans of Celsius feature cheerful images of fresh fruit, graphics of people working out and the inspirational slogan “Live Fit” – nothing like the warring bulls or grisly claw marks on Red Bull and Monster cans. There’s no mention of caffeine on the front of the can, and instead of “energy drink”, Celsius uses the gentler phrase “essential energy”.

In other words, it is in my view that Celsius as a “better for you” category of energy drinks could capture a larger share of the growth in the industry. Celsius' brand image could serve as a moat against “sugar-free” versions of Red Bull or Monster. This sentiment was echoed by management in their last earnings call, where they pointed out that 47% of new dollar industry growth in the Quarter can be attributed to Celsius. This implies that Celsius is bringing in new customers into the category and does not necessarily need to directly steal market share from Monster or Red Bull to continue on its growth trajectory. According to management in its earnings call;

Despite systematic and unanticipated category growth pressure in the second quarter, Celsius was resilient and delivered delicious new innovation, expanded in-store shelf presence and continued to bring new consumers into the category, contributing 47% of all category growth in the quarter, making Celsius the clear category growth leader.

Earnings Preview

Given the difficult Nielsen data in the short term, all eyes should be on the company’s upcoming earnings release in early November. In particular, investors would like to see if Celsius could maintain its high-growth rates despite the overall market slowdown for energy drinks.

Last quarter Q2, 2024, the company reported revenue of $402.0 million. This was a 23% year-over-year increase. Revenue for the first half of 2024 was $757.7 million. This translated to an increase of 29% compared to $585.8 million for the prior-year period. Most of Celsius revenue is generated in the US as international revenue $19.6 or 5% of total revenue

In this upcoming earnings, Investors will examine revenue to make sure that it is on the right track. CELH stock could drop even lower if expectations are not met. There are two key things to watch out for. The first is that Revenue growth could slow down if PepsiCo (PEP) does not immediately refresh its inventory. Remember that Celsius recognizes revenue upon delivery to Pepsi, not when it is bought by consumers.

If Pepsi’s management feels that the category growth is slowing down, it may play it safe and not immediately stock up on Celsius energy drinks. In other words, Celsius products could still be flying off the shelves, yet Pepsi management may not immediately restock or wait until inventory levels are down. The company’s management expects Pepsi to reduce its orders by $100M-$120 million in Q3, 2024.

Management has a chance to offset this lost revenue by leaning in and focusing on International growth. The company has made plans to distribute in the UK, Canada, and France in the very near future. The company’s main distribution partner in Europe is the Suntory group. Australia and New Zealand are expected to begin distribution in Q4 2024 while distribution in the United Kingdom and Ireland has already begun. I believe this could be a significant driver of revenue growth for the company in the coming months.

Valuation and Conclusion

In terms of valuation, CELH stock may seem expensive even with the price drop. The company is trading at 5.26x price to sales, compared to the median of 1.29x price to sales for the consumer staples sector. However, when strictly isolating to its direct competitors in the non-alcoholic beverages sector, Celsius actually isn’t wildly expensive. On a price-to-sales basis, Celsius is “cheaper” than Monster or Coke, which are trading at a price-to-sales of 7.5x and 6.55x sales respectively.

Looking at Celsius’ earnings isn’t really as important in my opinion, as it is a growing company. Over the long term, I believe that Celsius will have similar earnings margins with Monster and Coke once it reaches scale. Therefore, I believe that Celsius is reasonably priced at this level. Given the severity of the stock’s drop in price, I believe that all the negative news has already been priced in. CELH is still a buy for me.

data.title: Schrödinger, Inc. Continues To Be A Complicated Story
data.description:
Today, we put Schrödinger, Inc. (NASDAQ:SDGR) in the spotlight. The company has two distinct business segments. One that is seeing solid and consistent growth and the other where business is much “lumpier.” Not surprisingly, the company draws mixed views from analyst firms. Until the overall market, Schrödinger's stock has had a rough go of it in 2024 with the shares down by half. Is the equity now significantly in 'oversold' territory? An analysis follows below.

Schrödinger, Inc. is headquartered in New York City. As mentioned above, the company has two main business divisions. They are Software and Drug Discovery. The former develops and maintains software for drug discovery for primarily the life sciences and industrial materials industries, which enables it to develop products at a lower cost with a higher success rate. The stock currently trades around $18.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $1.3 billion.

The Drug Discovery side of the business has several compounds in development, both wholly owned and in collaboration deals with the likes of larger drugmakers like Gilead Sciences (GILD). The company currently has eight wholly owned compounds in development. However, all of them are very early stage and will not be germane to this analysis.

The pipeline of collaboration candidates is more advanced and more interesting. It consists of 17 assets, one of which is approved and several in mid to late-stage development.

Recent Results:

Schrödinger, Inc. posted its Q2 numbers on July 31st. It was a solid earnings report on a headline basis. The company posted a non-GAAP loss of 66 cents a share, 33 cents a share above the consensus. Operating expenses rose to $84.1 million from $74.9 million in the second quarter of the previous year, primarily due to an increase in R&D costs.

Revenues rose just over 34% on a year-over-year basis to $47.3 million, nearly $8 million over expectations. Software sales were the bulk of overall revenues, at $35.4 million for the quarter. This was up a solid 21% over the same period in FY2023. Drug Discovery revenue was $11.9 million compared to $5.8 million in 2Q2023, due to the timing of developmental milestone payouts.

Leadership also provided the following updated FY2024 guidance.

Shortly after the second quarter close, Schrödinger announced an initiative to expand its computational platform to predict the toxicity associated with binding to off-targets. This effort is initially funded by a $10 million grant from the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Analyst firms are split down the middle around the current prospects for Schrödinger. Since second quarter results hit the wires, Leerink Partners ($30 price target, up from $29 previously), Craig-Hallum and TD Cowen ($2 price target) have reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Meanwhile, Bank of America, Morgan Stanley ($30 price target) and Goldman Sachs ($27 price target) have maintained their Hold ratings on the stock.

Approximately 14% of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. Insider activity in the stock is sparse so far in 2024. Several insiders sold just under $200,000 worth of share collectively in February and early March. However, those have been the only insider transactions in the equity year to date.

Schrödinger, Inc. closed the first half of 2024 with just over $380 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. It ended FY2023 with just under $470 million worth of marketable securities and cash, it should be noted. The company listed no long-term debt on the 10-Q it filed for the second quarter. In addition, in early July, Morphic Holding, Inc. (MORF) was purchased by drug giant Eli Lilly (LLY) for $57 a share. At the time of the announcement, Schrödinger held nearly 835,000 shares in MORF, worth just over $47 million.

Conclusion:

Schrödinger, Inc. posted profits of 54 cents a share in FY2023 on just under $217 million worth of revenues. However, the current analyst firm consensus sees a loss of just over $2.40 a share in FY2024 as revenues fall slightly to $210 million. They project similar losses in FY2025, even as sales grow in the mid-20s.

When I last looked at this company in April 2023, I stated Schrödinger was an interesting story to watch, but I was passing on any investment recommendation on the stock at that time. The stock was trading in the mid $20s at the time, so that was the right call.

The challenge with Schrödinger, Inc. stock is it is difficult to value. The software business is fairly straightforward. It is seeing solid and consistent growth and if one puts a valuation of 3-5 times price to annual sales range on it is worth between $420 million to $700 million. Add in the current net cash of approximately $380 million, and you get a combined value of around $950 million, using the midpoint of the value range on the software side. That puts a value of just $350 million on the Drug Discovery side of the business.

What are the eight wholly owned and 17 partnered compounds worth is the question? Especially given the company is likely to print losses over at least the next couple of fiscal years. Given this valuation challenge, it is easy to see why the analyst community is firmly split around Schrödinger, Inc.'s current prospects. With the stock trading right at 52-week lows, I think there is enough here that I have taken a very small “watch item” position in this complicated name via covered call orders, which help mitigate further downside risk.

I will likely circle back on Schrödinger, Inc. in late 2025 or early 2026 to do another update on this potentially attractive “sum of the parts” story. A couple of the company's wholly owned candidates should be advanced enough by then to take a more in-depth look at them.

data.title: SMLV: No Reason Pairing Small-Cap With Low Volatility
data.description:
The SPDR SSGA US Small Cap Low Volatility Index ETF (NYSEARCA:SMLV), launched on 02/20/2013 by State Street Global Advisors and managed by SSGA Funds Management, is an ETF that tracks the performance of the SSGA US Small Cap Low Volatility Index.

The index measures the performance of stocks within the small-cap segment that exhibit low volatility. It starts with an available universe of 2000 small-cap U.S. stocks and then includes 30% of the stocks within each sector that rank at the top when it comes to the lowest volatility as measured by their standard deviation of monthly price returns over the trailing 5 years.

The ETF has $200 million under management, charges an expense ratio of 0.12%, and its turnover during its last fiscal year was 31%.

The idea here is to provide exposure to the small-cap segment of the equity market for those who are looking for more steady growth because they are either defensive at the moment or generally don't feel like taking a lot of risk. In neither of those cases, does SMLV seem like a good option, however. In this article, I will make a few comparisons when it comes to performance and volatility to show you why a large-cap low-volatility combination makes more sense.

Performance

Since it was launched, SMLV has realized an annualized return of 10.06%, which is very close to the 10.18% return reflected by the index.

To see how it has performed against a small-cap fund without a low-volatility bias, I chose the SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (SPSM) and the results were interesting. It seems that when you remove this factor, there is no significant difference. The returns are nearly identical, which is a good thing for SMVL, but its standard deviation is not that much lower and its maximum drawdown is almost the same. As a result, it hasn't resulted in a much better Sharpe.

As far as the inclusion of small-cap stocks in a portfolio is concerned, it doesn't seem to make much sense to choose a fund that aims at low volatility. As far as a defensive tilt is concerned, mid-cap and large-cap low-volatility funds seem better choices. Notably, the SPDR SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility ETF (LGLV) outperformed both its small-cap and mid-cap low-volatility counterparts and had almost the same Sharpe as that of the market, but with lower volatility, making it the superior choice when it comes to a defensive option:

After looking at the recent price performance of SMVL against that of LGLV, it becomes apparent that the latter is better at delivering the "promise" of the low-volatility factor to a meaningful degree. SMLV's 2022 drawdown that followed the start of the last Fed Funds rate hike cycle is about the same as that of LGLV, but LGLV has witnessed its price increase more since the Fed started raising the policy interest rate:

Risks

While the fund comes with small-cap risk, the primary risk that should concern most investors is a potential opportunity cost.

It seems that because of the federal funds rate cut of 50 bps, there is a decent chance the market should expect the following cuts to be of the same magnitude. This is unlikely, as the Fed should be aware of the risk of reflation if it moves too fast. Its commitment to decreasing its treasury holdings is a good indication of that awareness. Due to the market valuation right now, a correction isn't unlikely as the Fed moves more slowly going forward. So the current conditions are begging for a cautious approach.

SMLV may seem like a good fund to hold right now, as its earnings multiple and P/B ratio are 14.48x and 1.51x, respectively. But the potential missed opportunity in case the correction isn't realized makes it unfit. I think with large-cap low-volatility exposure, you can have your cake and eat it too.

Also, the ETF has a very high exposure to financials at 68.21% of the portfolio, introducing significant sector concentration risk.

Verdict

Small-cap exposure comes with higher risk and having a low-volatility bias hasn't proven to be a solution due to its limited impact on standard deviation, maximum drawdowns, and risk-adjusted returns. For this reason, I am rating SMLV a hold.

What's your opinion? Do you own this one, or do you prefer another way to invest right now? Let me know below! Thank you for reading.
